name: Respondents who consider a farming practice very unacceptable, weighted to match US demographics
descriptionShort: Counts are weighted by age, gender, race, income, and region to match US Census demographics.
datasetName: Public Acceptability Of Standard U.S. Animal Agriculture Practices

presentation.titlePublic: Respondents who consider a farming practice very unacceptable, weighted to match US demographics

descriptionKey: Survey participants were asked about the acceptability of 12 standard farming practices in the U.S. The responses were categorized into five levels: 'Very acceptable', 'Somewhat acceptable', 'Neither acceptable nor unacceptable', 'Somewhat unacceptable', and 'Very unacceptable'.
Weighted counts are adjusted by age, gender, race, income, and region to match U.S. Census demographics.

List of farming practices considered, as described in the survey:
* Keeping chickens inside a barn with no outdoor access and with less than one square foot of space per bird.
* Growing chickens to reach market weight size by 47 days, leading to difficulties with walking and standing.
* Hanging live chickens upside down by their legs before stunning them in electrified water, slitting their throats, and finally submerging them in boiling water.
* Castrating new-born calves by surgically removing the testicles, almost always without anesthesia or pain-killers.
* Removing calves' horn buds (undeveloped horn tissue before it grows into a visible horn) using a knife or hot iron, sometimes without anesthesia or pain-killers.
* Permanently separating calves from their mothers immediately after birth.
* Cutting the beaks off new-born chickens, almost always without anesthesia or pain-killers.
* Killing new-born male chicks who can't lay eggs by use of meat-grinders.
* Keeping chickens in cages with 67-86 square inches of space per bird (smaller than a standard sheet of letter paper).
* Keeping pigs in cages which prevent them from turning around for several weeks.
* Killing pigs in gas chambers by use of carbon dioxide (CO2) gas.
* Cutting the tails off new-born piglets, almost always without anesthesia or pain-killers.

values.name: Calves are permanently separated from their mothers at birth
Calves' horn buds are removed with a knife or hot iron, sometimes without pain relief
Chickens are bred to grow fast and struggle to walk and stand
Chickens are kept in cages, with less room than a sheet of paper per bird
Chickens are kept indoors with little space per bird
Live chickens are hung upside down, stunned, have their throats slit, then boiled
Newborn calves are castrated without pain relief
Newborn chickens' beaks are cut off without pain relief
Newborn male chicks are killed in meat-grinders
Newborn piglets' tails are cut off without pain relief
Pigs are kept in cages, unable to turn around for weeks
Pigs are killed in CO₂ gas chambers

origins.title: Public Acceptability Of Standard U.S. Animal Agriculture Practices
origins.description: Survey of approximately 1,000 U.S. adults on their perceptions of standard animal agriculture practices.
origins.citationFull: Polanco, A., & Troy, A. (2025). Public Acceptability Of Standard U.S. Animal Agriculture Practices. Faunalytics.
